description: A surface, material, or environment is 'kurathi' when it has little moisture. Dry grain often keeps well, while dry soil may need water; the word reports the condition without deciding whether it is useful. 'Kuramo' (drought) names a prolonged water deficit, not one dry object or afternoon.
articulatory_notes: The back of the tongue closes against the soft palate for 'k' and releases into rounded high-back 'u'. A tap or short trill begins the stressed second syllable before open central 'a'. The tongue then approaches the upper teeth for voiceless 'th' before high-front 'i'.
usage_notes: A dry tree is 'kurathi shiro'; dry ash is 'kurathi hesura'. A thirsty living being is 'kishu' (thirsty), which states bodily need rather than the moisture in a material.

semantic_domains.nature: low moisture in air, soil, plants, or weather
semantic_domains.physical: little liquid present on a surface or within a material
